abstract: Neuroscience has historically served as a foundational source of inspiration for artificial intelligence (AI), contributing to breakthroughs in neural network architectures and reinforcement learning. However, as modern AI increasingly diverges from biological principles, it continues to struggle with the robust sensorimotor capabilities and behavioral flexibility observed in even simple animals. We argue that the next generation of AI will require a renewed commitment to NeuroAI—a multidisciplinary field dedicated to distilling the computational principles of the brain. Central to this initiative is the "embodied Turing test," a challenge that task-shifts the benchmark of intelligence from abstract linguistic or strategic performance to the ability of artificial agents to interact with the physical world at a level indistinguishable from living organisms. By focusing on the evolutionary foundations of intelligence—such as efficient motor control and rapid adaptation—researchers can leverage diverse biological datasets to build more capable, energy-efficient, and generalizable systems. Scaling this research will necessitate significant investment in dual-trained scientists, shared high-performance computing platforms, and fundamental research into how integrated neural circuits generate complex behavior. Achieving animal-level sensorimotor intelligence will provide the essential scaffolding for reaching true human-level artificial general intelligence.
